result:
**No — `eloop.c` uses no locks, mutexes, or RCU of any kind.** It's a purely single-threaded event loop; the timeout list and socket registration tables are plain unsynchronized data structures.

Evidence gathered:

- No `pthread.h` include and no `pthread_*`/mutex/semaphore/atomic/RCU symbols anywhere in the file (`grep` for `pthread|mutex|lock|rcu|atomic|spinlock|sem_` returns nothing relevant — only an unrelated comment mentioning "blocking").
- Timeout list: `eloop.timeout` is a plain intrusive doubly-linked list (`struct dl_list`, from `list.h`), manipulated with unsynchronized `dl_list_add`, `dl_list_add_tail`, `dl_list_del`, `dl_list_for_each` / `_safe` in `eloop_register_timeout`, `eloop_remove_timeout`, `eloop_cancel_timeout`, etc. (eloop.c:640-733).
- Socket registration: `eloop.readers` / `eloop.writers` / `eloop.exceptions` are `struct eloop_sock_table` holding a plain `struct eloop_sock *table` array (realloc'd via `os_realloc`), guarded only by an `int count` — no synchronization primitive protects concurrent add/remove.
- The global state itself, `struct eloop_data eloop`, is a single static struct (eloop.c:99) with plain `int` fields (`count`, `signal_count`, `signaled`, `pending_terminate`, `terminate`) — not even declared `volatile sig_atomic_t`.
- Signal handling (the one place concurrency could matter) uses the classic Unix pattern instead of locking: `eloop_handle_signal()` (registered via plain `signal()`, eloop.c:803-824) only increments counters (`eloop.signaled++`, `eloop.signals[i].signaled++`). The actual dispatch that walks/mutates lists, `eloop_process_pending_signals()`, runs later from the normal main-loop context (not from within the signal handler), so list mutation never happens concurrently with itself or with the poll/select/epoll wait.
- `SIGSEGV`/`SIGALRM` handlers similarly just set a flag or call `exit()`/`abort()` — they never touch the timeout/socket lists.

In short: eloop is designed to run entirely on one thread; safety against reentrancy comes from deferring all list-mutating work out of async signal handlers into the main loop, not from any lock/mutex/RCU mechanism.
